skill.name: linux-troubleshooting
skill.description: Linux system troubleshooting workflow for diagnosing and resolving system issues, performance problems, and service failures.
skill.summary: Structured workflow for Linux system diagnosis covering resource analysis, log review, network diagnostics, and service troubleshooting.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 47 static analysis findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file is documentation-only (markdown) containing workflow instructions and example commands. The detected 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown code fence markers (```bash), not Ruby/shell backticks. The 'hardcoded URL' and 'reconnaissance' patterns are documented examples for users, not executable code. No actual security risks detected.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill documentation includes example shell commands for system diagnostics (uptime, top, df, journalctl, etc.). These are intended for user execution via other skills, not direct code execution by this skill.

content.user_title: Diagnose and Resolve Linux System Issues
content.value_statement: Linux administrators face complex system failures requiring structured diagnosis. This workflow provides a seven-phase approach to systematically identify root causes and implement verified fixes.
actual_capabilities: Guide systematic diagnosis through seven structured phases from assessment to resolution
Coordinate with specialized skills for bash commands, performance analysis, and error detection
Provide copy-paste prompts for invoking related troubleshooting skills
Document findings and solutions for future reference and prevention
limitations: This is a workflow guide only - requires invocation of other skills to execute commands
Does not directly execute shell commands or access system resources
Requires user to have appropriate system access permissions for diagnostic commands
Network diagnostics require external connectivity and may not work in isolated environments

use_cases.title: Production Server Outage Response
use_cases.description: Follow the seven-phase workflow to diagnose why a production server became unresponsive, identify the root cause (resource exhaustion, service failure, or network issue), and implement a verified fix.
use_cases.target_user: Site reliability engineer responding to PagerDuty alerts

use_cases.title: Performance Degradation Investigation
use_cases.description: Use resource analysis and process investigation phases to identify which processes are consuming excessive CPU or memory, then work with server-management skill to resolve the issue.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineer investigating slow application performance

use_cases.title: Service Failure Diagnosis
use_cases.description: Apply service troubleshooting phase to diagnose why systemd services fail to start, review logs with error-detective skill, and implement configuration fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic System Health Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the linux-troubleshooting workflow to check system health. Start with Phase 1 (Initial Assessment) and Phase 2 (Resource Analysis). Run uptime, check CPU and memory usage with top and free, and report disk space with df -h.

prompt_templates.title: Service Not Starting
prompt_templates.prompt: A critical service is failing to start. Follow Phase 6 (Service Troubleshooting) to check systemctl status, review logs with journalctl -u service -f, and identify configuration issues. Then use Phase 4 (Log Analysis) to search for related errors in /var/log/.

prompt_templates.title: Network Connectivity Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Users cannot reach our web server. Execute Phase 5 (Network Diagnostics) to check network interfaces with ip addr, verify listening ports with ss -tulpn, test connectivity with curl, and check DNS resolution with dig. Correlate findings with firewall rules.

prompt_templates.title: Full Incident Response
prompt_templates.prompt: Production server is experiencing critical issues. Run the complete seven-phase linux-troubleshooting workflow: (1) Initial Assessment, (2) Resource Analysis, (3) Process Investigation, (4) Log Analysis, (5) Network Diagnostics, (6) Service Troubleshooting, (7) Resolution. Document findings at each phase and implement verified fixes.

output_examples.input: Server is running slowly. Start troubleshooting.
output: - Recent kernel update detected in dmesg
Recommended action: Investigate Java process memory leak and clear disk space in /var/log

output_examples.input: nginx service failed to start after config change
output: - systemctl status nginx: Failed with exit code 1
- journalctl -u nginx: 'nginx: [emerg] unknown directive' at line 47
- Configuration test: nginx -t shows syntax error in /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default
Root cause: Invalid directive introduced in recent config edit
Fix: Restore previous config from backup and reload nginx

best_practices: Always document findings at each phase before proceeding to the next
Verify fixes by re-running diagnostic commands to confirm resolution
Create prevention plans after resolution to avoid recurring issues
anti_patterns: Skipping phases and jumping directly to restarts without diagnosing root cause
Implementing fixes without first verifying the identified root cause
Failing to monitor system stability after applying resolution

faq.question: What skills do I need to use this workflow?
faq.answer: This workflow coordinates with bash-linux for command execution, performance-engineer for resource analysis, server-management for process and service management, error-detective for log analysis, and network-engineer for network diagnostics. Each phase specifies which skills to invoke.

faq.question: Can this workflow automatically fix issues?
faq.answer: No, this is a structured workflow guide. It provides systematic diagnosis steps and coordinates with other skills to execute commands. All fixes require user approval before implementation.

faq.question: What if I only need to check one specific area?
faq.answer: You can execute individual phases independently. For example, run only Phase 5 for network issues or Phase 4 for log analysis. The workflow is modular and each phase is self-contained.

faq.question: How do I document the troubleshooting process?
faq.answer: Phase 1 includes gathering initial findings, and Phase 7 includes creating resolution documentation. Each phase has a 'Document findings' step. The workflow encourages maintaining a troubleshooting log throughout the process.

faq.question: What permissions do I need to run these diagnostics?
faq.answer: Most diagnostic commands (top, df, ss) work with standard user permissions. Some commands like journalctl -xe, strace, and service management require sudo/root access. Ensure your Claude Code session has appropriate system permissions.

faq.question: Can I use this workflow for containerized environments?
faq.answer: Yes, but some commands may behave differently inside containers. Commands like systemctl may not work in containers without systemd. Adapt the workflow by using container-specific alternatives (docker top, docker logs) where applicable.
